perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and how ever disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, draw ings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performan ce results relating to the
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means only that data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is explicitly marked as 'Confidential' at the time of disclosure, whether in tangible form, whenever and how ever disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, draw ings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performan ce results relating to the
perturbation.explanation: The modified text restricts the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only data explicitly marked as such. The original agreement defined it more broadly. This creates an in-text contradiction with section 1.3 which states 'Confidential Information shall be identified either by marking it, in the case of wri t- ten materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by n otifying the Recipient of the confidential nature of the info r- mation.' since it implies that unmarked information, if notified, could still be considered confidential. This creates confusion and uncertainty about what information is actually protected under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.3 Confidential Information shall be identified either by marking it, in the case of wri t- ten materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by n otifying the Recipient of the confidential nature of the info r- mation. Such notification shall be done orally, by e -mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 1.1 to define 'Confidential Information' as only that data explicitly marked as 'Confidential'. This directly contradicts section 1.3, which states that Confidential Information can be identified either by marking it or, if unmarked, by notifying the recipient of its confidential nature. The change creates a clear inconsistency because section 1.3 allows for unmarked information to be considered confidential under certain conditions, which is not allowed under the new definition of Confidential Information in section 1.1.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Recipient will not disclos e and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agent s who need to have access to the Co n- fidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Recipient will inform them about the co n- fidential quality of the information provide d and will ensure that their agreement is o b- tained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained he rein will be s trictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Recipient may disclos e the information received to its employees, representatives or agent s, regardless of their need to have access to the Co n- fidential Information. The Recipient is not responsible for informing them about the co n- fidential quality of the information provide d and is not responsible for ensuring that their agreement is o b- tained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will not be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained he rein will be s trictly observed and will not assume any liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents .
perturbation.explanation: The modified text now allows the Recipient to disclose confidential information to anyone without needing a reason or ensuring confidentiality. This is an in-text contradiction with the entire purpose of the NDA, particularly section 3.10, which states 'The Recipient agree s that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Conf i- dential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement'. This contradiction creates a situation where the recipient is simultaneously obligated to protect information and allowed to disclose it freely.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.10 The Recipient agree s that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Conf i- dential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 3.2 explicitly states that the Recipient is not responsible for maintaining confidentiality or ensuring their personnel adhere to the agreement's terms, directly contradicting section 3.10. Section 3.10 emphasizes that any disclosure of Confidential Information constitutes a breach of the agreement and would cause irreparable damage to the Discloser, entitling them to injunctive relief and damages. The change in 3.2 allows for disclosure without accountability, creating a clear and strong in-text contradiction by undermining the fundamental principle of confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.2 If the Discloser and Recipient succeed in the call for proposal referred to in clause 2 and sign the corresponding Grant Agreement (GA) and Consortium Agreement (CA), or entered into partnership under any other kind of collaborative agreement (COA) or ass o- ciation agreement (AA) , the non -disclosure provisions of the CA, COA and AA shall [sup- plement or supersede ] this Agreement. In the event that non -disclosure provisions are not provided for the said private agreements in equal terms as stated herein , this Agreement shall remain in force until the end of the collaboration undert aken or after [months or years ] of its termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.2 If the Discloser and Recipient succeed in the call for proposal referred to in clause 2 and sign the corresponding Grant Agreement (GA) and Consortium Agreement (CA), or entered into partnership under any other kind of collaborative agreement (COA) or ass o- ciation agreement (AA) , the non -disclosure provisions of the CA, COA and AA shall not impact this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in force until the end of the collaboration undert aken or after [months or years ] of its termination.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text states that the non-disclosure provisions of any subsequent agreements (CA, COA, AA) will not impact the existing NDA. The original text had those agreements potentially 'supplementing or superseding' the current agreement. This in-text contradiction creates uncertainty with Section 4.1.1 which talks about the period of confidentiality of the recipient. The interaction between these clauses can lead to legal disputes about the level of confidentiality that the recipient must ensure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 Duration and Termination 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ] term. Notwithstanding th e foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect for a period of one (1) year after the termination of this agreement, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for subsequent agreements (CA, COA, AA) to supplement or supersede the current agreement, implying changes to the NDA's terms. The changed text explicitly states these subsequent agreements will *not* impact the current agreement. Section 4.1.1 defines the duration of confidentiality, including a one-year period after termination. If a subsequent agreement (as permitted in the original text, but prohibited in the changed text) had different confidentiality terms, it could affect the duration outlined in 4.1.1. Therefore, the change introduces a direct contradiction because the fixed confidentiality term established in 4.1.1 could be superseded under the original text, while the changed text disallows any change from subsequent agreements. This satisfies the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The Recipient will use the Confidential Information exclusively for the permitted pu r- pose stated in clause 2 and not use the in formation for its own purposes or benefit.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 The Recipient will use the Confidential Information for any purpose, including for its own purposes or benefit.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text states that the Recipient can use the confidential information for any purpose, which directly contradicts the original clause that restricted its use to only the purposes stated in clause 2. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3.1, 'The Recipient agrees to use the Confidential Information solely in connectio n with purposes contemplat ed in this Agreement and not to use it for any other purpose or without the prior written consent of the Discloser .' This contradiction undermines the entire foundation of the NDA, which is to protect the Discloser's confidential information from unauthorized use.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 In the context of discussions, preparations or negotiations, the Discloser may disclose Confidential Information to the Recipient . The Recipient agrees to use the Confidential Information solely in connectio n with purposes contemplat ed in this Agreement and not to use it for any other purpose or without the prior written consent of the Discloser .
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 3.3 now allows the Recipient to use Confidential Information for 'any purpose,' which directly conflicts with section 3.1. Section 3.1 restricts the use of Confidential Information 'solely in connection with purposes contemplated in this Agreement and not to use it for any other purpose.' This is a clear and direct contradiction regarding the permitted use of Confidential Information, undermining the confidentiality obligations and meeting the definition of inconsistencies leading to in-text contradictions.
